chapter: Ch 8 — The One Idea: Sum-Check and GKR
stratum: Algebra II: multivariate & multilinear (the modern representation)

phases.pre_rigorous: Picture a prosecutor facing a suspect who claims an airtight alibi that covers an impossible number of moments at once — every minute of a whole week. The prosecutor cannot replay the week. So instead of checking everything, she narrows: 'Account for Monday.' The suspect must give a consistent sub-story; she picks a random hour inside it, pins it down, and asks him to account for THAT — then a random minute inside that. A truthful suspect's story survives every narrowing; a liar's must eventually contradict itself at a moment he could not have anticipated. Sum-check is exactly this. The prover claims a single number H is the total of g over all 2³ = 8 corners of the cube {0,1}³ — too many to add for real n. Lead with the figure (the running claim plotted as the ladder narrows: 8 terms folded into the claim, then 4, then 2, then 1). Each round the prover hands over a small one-variable story g_i; the verifier checks it splits consistently into its two halves, then drops a random finger r_i and demands the smaller story. After three narrowings nothing is left to add — just one point to check. (No formal notation yet beyond naming g and H — the 'why' comes before the symbols.)
phases.rigorous: Now earn it. Let g be a polynomial in v variables over a finite field F, with per-variable degree at most d, and let H = Σ_{x∈{0,1}^v} g(x) be the claimed sum. ROUND i: the prover sends the univariate g_i(X) = Σ over the remaining Boolean variables of g, with x_1..x_{i−1} fixed to the past challenges r_1..r_{i−1} and x_i = X. COMPLETENESS: an honest prover's g_i satisfies g_i(0)+g_i(1) = (running claim), since splitting variable i into its two Boolean values is exactly the next step of the sum; round 1 checks against H, round i>1 against g_{i−1}(r_{i−1}). The verifier checks that one identity, draws r_i uniformly at random, and recurses on the (v−1)-variable claim g_i(r_i). After v rounds it checks the lone value g_v(r_v) = g(r_1,…,r_v) against g directly. SOUNDNESS (induction on rounds + Schwartz–Zippel): if the prover lies, its g_i differs from the true round polynomial; the difference is a NON-ZERO polynomial of degree ≤ d, so a uniformly random r_i makes them disagree — exposing the lie — with probability ≥ 1 − d/|F|. To keep cheating the prover must, at each round, send a wrong g_i that nonetheless satisfies the consistency check, which forces it into a fresh false claim it again cannot defend. By the union bound the total chance a liar slips through all v rounds is ≤ d·v/|F|. This destroys the bad intuitions explicitly: (1) 'verify a sum ⇒ recompute it' — false, the verifier checks v consistency identities plus one evaluation, never the 2^v terms; (2) 'the verifier knows the true partial sums' — false, it only compares g_i(0)+g_i(1) to the PREVIOUS reduced claim; (3) 'a caught liar repairs the lie next round' — false, the lie must survive every round; (4) 'sum-check needs a commitment' — false, the only ingredient is Schwartz–Zippel and oracle access to g, so soundness is unconditional. For our instance over GF(97), d = 2 (x1 is squared), v = 3, so the worst-case bound is 2·3/97 = 6/97 ≈ 6.2%.
phases.post_rigorous: Rebuild the intuition on the rigor, both halves at once. The prosecutor's 'narrow the confession until one checkable fact remains' IS the round reduction n → n−1 ending at g(r_1,…,r_v); 'the lie must contradict itself at a random moment' IS Schwartz–Zippel making a wrong univariate non-zero at the random r_i; 'a truthful story survives every narrowing' IS completeness. Pair each picture with its statement (see the table in draft.md). Now the structure feels inevitable: the verifier's work is v small univariate checks plus one evaluation — exponential sum, linear-in-v verification — and the soundness knob is the same d·v/|F| Schwartz–Zippel dial from Ch 7, only summed over rounds. Two boundaries stay sharp. First, sum-check is a PURE reduction: it assumes oracle access to g and uses NO commitment, group, or hardness assumption — exactly the purity that makes GKR the first no-commitment proof system the reader meets, and the reason a commitment scheme only enters later (Ch 9) when g itself must be pinned down. Second, sum-check by itself is NOT zero-knowledge: it reveals genuine evaluations of g; masking comes in Ch 9–10. This single gadget is the engine beneath Layer 4 and the most-reused move in the rest of the book — grand-product/ZeroTest (Ch 9), Spartan and the folding hinge (Ch 10), folding schemes defined on sum-check (Ch 14), LogUp-GKR and zkVM lookups (Ch 15).
phases.you_could_have_invented_this: Knowing only that (a) a degree-≤d univariate is pinned down by a few evaluations and that g_i(0)+g_i(1) should equal the running claim, and (b) a non-zero degree-≤d polynomial is non-zero at a random point with probability ≥ 1 − d/|F|, you can invent sum-check: strip one variable per round, ask for the univariate g_i, check the split identity, pin a random r_i, recurse, and finish with one evaluation g(r_1,…,r_v). Honest provers always pass; a cheat survives with probability ≤ d·v/|F|. You derived it; you did not receive it — and you used no commitment, only Schwartz–Zippel.

heuristic_rigorous_pairs: A prosecutor narrows a confession round by round until one checkable fact remains.
The round reduction sends a v-variable claim H = Σ_{x∈{0,1}^v} g(x) to a (v−1)-variable claim g_i(r_i), and after v rounds to the single evaluation g(r_1,…,r_v).
The liar's story must contradict itself at a random moment he could not anticipate.
A lying g_i differs from the true round polynomial by a non-zero polynomial of degree ≤ d, so a uniformly random r_i exposes it with probability ≥ 1 − d/|F| (Schwartz–Zippel).
A truthful story survives every narrowing.
Completeness: an honest prover's g_i satisfies g_i(0)+g_i(1) = running claim, because splitting variable i into 0 and 1 is exactly the next term of the sum.
Being caught once is enough; luck does not compound into safety.
Soundness is an induction on rounds; the per-round risks ≤ d/|F| ADD by the union bound to a total ≤ d·v/|F| (linear in v), not a product that blows up.
The verifier never adds the eight cube corners (figure: 8 → 4 → 2 → 1).
For g over GF(97), H = 54; the verifier checks g1(0)+g1(1)=54, then the chain 54 → g1(2)=47 → g2(3)=71 → g3(4)=67 = g(2,3,4), v=3 univariate checks plus one evaluation.

claims.claim: The field is GF(97) (|F| = 97) and g(x1,x2,x3) = 2·x1²·x2 + 3·x2·x3 + x1 + 5 is a 3-variable polynomial over it.
claims.evidence: Sage manifest field = 97, num_vars = 3; the manim scene SCENE_VALUES.field = 97, num_vars = 3 and reconstructs g in pure Python.

claims.claim: The claimed sum over the Boolean hypercube is H = Σ_{x∈{0,1}³} g(x) = 54.
claims.evidence: Sage manifest H = 54; an independent Sage re-derivation and the manim scene's pure-Python recompute both give 54.
All four sum-check identities are asserted in the Sage recipe (it cannot emit a manifest unless they hold) and reproduced in a second Sage session and in the manim scene.

claims.claim: Final fold (r3=4): g3(4) = 67 = g(2,3,4) = 67 — the single evaluation checked against g directly.
claims.evidence: Sage: g3(4)=67 and g(2,3,4)=67; identity g3(r3)==g(r1,r2,r3) asserted in the recipe; scene final_eval = 67.

claims.claim: The soundness bound is d·v/|F| = 2·3/97 = 6/97 ≈ 6.2% (d = per-variable degree bound, v = variables, |F| field size).
claims.source: lemma (Schwartz–Zippel per round + union bound) + sage_manifest.soundness_bound
claims.evidence: Each round risks ≤ d/|F| = 2/97; union bound over v=3 rounds gives ≤ 6/97 ≈ 0.0619. Worst case (d=2 because x1 is squared); realized round degrees 2,1,1 give ≤ 4/97 — stated separately.
claims.ledger_ref: CHAPTER_BIBLE Ch 8 'soundness bound d·v/|F| ... derived from Ch 7's Schwartz-Zippel, one round at a time.'

claims.claim: Sum-check uses no commitment scheme and no hardness assumption; soundness is unconditional given oracle access to g.
claims.source: CHAPTER_BIBLE Ch 8 'Connects backward' + graphify path to Schwartz-Zippel
claims.evidence: The only soundness ingredient is Schwartz–Zippel applied per round; no group, commitment, or assumption appears. Bible: 'sum-check needs no commitment scheme — a deliberate purity that makes GKR the first practical no-commitment proof system.'

scene_value_check.note: Empty list = no drift; every numeric value the manim scene displays matches the Sage manifest.

adversarial_findings.finding: Univariate degrees differ across rounds (g1 degree 2; g2,g3 degree 1); a reader could think the bound 6/97 with d=2 is wrong.
adversarial_findings.resolution: d is the per-variable degree BOUND (max possible univariate degree, here 2 since x1 is squared); d·v/|F| uses this uniform upper bound. Realized degrees (2,1,1) only lower the true risk (≤ 4/97). Both stated separately in draft.md.

adversarial_findings.finding: Off-by-one / transcription risk in the running-claim chain H → g1(r1) → g2(r2) → g3(r3).
adversarial_findings.resolution: All four identities are Python asserts in the recipe (no manifest emitted unless they hold); re-derived in a second Sage session (54→47→71→67); the scene recomputes the chain and validate_scene_values returns [].

adversarial_findings.finding: Challenges are fixed (2,3,4), not random — could look unsound or value-dependent.
adversarial_findings.resolution: Fixed challenges are for a reproducible transcript (the completeness side); soundness is about a uniformly random r_i and is argued separately via Schwartz–Zippel. draft.md states r_i is random and unpredictable to the prover in a real run.

adversarial_findings.finding: Risk the figure/animation is empty or stale.
adversarial_findings.resolution: Re-ran the recipe; sum_check.svg is 40,778 bytes. Rendered with still=False; sum_check.mp4 is 770,050 bytes. Both copied into the draft and non-empty.

comprehension_set.question: In one round of sum-check, what does the prover send, and exactly which identity does the verifier check before moving on — and does the verifier ever recompute the true partial sum?
comprehension_set.answer_key: The prover sends a univariate g_i(X) (the claimed partial sum with earlier variables fixed to past challenges and later variables summed over {0,1}). The verifier checks g_i(0) + g_i(1) = (running claim) (H in round 1, g_{i−1}(r_{i−1}) afterward). It never recomputes a true partial sum; its only direct contact with g is the final g(r_1,…,r_n).
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: ‘Each round the verifier compares g_i to the TRUE univariate, so it must already know the answer’ — it only checks an internal consistency identity.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: MLE (multilinear extension) — the object summed over the Boolean hypercube

comprehension_set.question: For g(x1,x2,x3) = 2x1²x2 + 3x2x3 + x1 + 5 over GF(97) with H = 54 and fixed challenges r1=2, r2=3, r3=4: the prover sends g1(X) = 23 + 4X + 4X². Check round 1, then state the running claim entering round 2 and the final value the verifier checks against g directly.
comprehension_set.answer_key: Round 1: g1(0)+g1(1) = 23 + 31 = 54 = H ✓. Running claim entering round 2 = g1(2) = 47. Then g2(X)=14+19X gives 14+33=47=g1(2), g2(3)=71; g3(X)=31+9X gives 31+40=71=g2(3), g3(4)=67. The one direct check against g is g3(4)=67 = g(2,3,4)=67.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: ‘The verifier must look at all 2^n terms (or blindly trust H)’ — it checks a chain of one-variable identities ending in one evaluation.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: summation/expectation over a finite domain — the sum over {0,1}^n

comprehension_set.question: Why can a cheating prover not just repair its lie each round, and why is the total soundness error only d·v/|F| rather than something that blows up with the number of variables?
comprehension_set.answer_key: A lie forces g_i to differ from the true round polynomial by a non-zero degree-≤d polynomial, so a random r_i exposes it with probability ≥ 1 − d/|F| (Schwartz–Zippel), pushing the prover into a fresh undefendable claim; the lie must survive every round (induction). The per-round risks ≤ d/|F| ADD by the union bound over v variables to ≤ d·v/|F| — linear in v, tiny for a large field. No commitment or assumption is used.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: ‘Per-round error compounds multiplicatively / a caught liar can repair its lie next round’ — errors add to d·v/|F| and a lie must survive every round.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: the Schwartz–Zippel lemma — the per-round soundness source (carried from Ch 7)

comprehension_set.question: You could have invented this. You must convince a verifier that Σ_{x∈{0,1}^n} g(x) = H without anyone adding 2^n terms, knowing only that (a) a low-degree univariate is pinned down by a few evaluations and (b) a non-zero low-degree polynomial is non-zero at a random point with high probability. Design a protocol and give its error.
comprehension_set.answer_key: Strip one variable per round. Round i: ask for g_i(X) = Σ over the remaining {0,1} variables of g with x_1..x_{i−1} fixed to past challenges and x_i = X; check g_i(0)+g_i(1) = current claim; pick random r_i and demand the smaller claim g_i(r_i) next round (a lying g_i is exposed at random r_i with prob ≥ 1 − d/|F|). After n rounds check g_n(r_n) = g(r_1,…,r_n) directly. Honest provers always pass; a cheat survives all rounds with probability ≤ d·v/|F|. That is sum-check — no commitment, just Schwartz–Zippel per round.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: ‘Sum-check needs a commitment / cryptographic primitive’ — the construction uses only Schwartz–Zippel and oracle access to g.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: proof by mathematical induction — the soundness argument is an induction on rounds
